title: High Speed CAN Communication Bus Performance
system: High Speed CAN Communication Bus Performance
possible_causes: High Speed CAN Communication Bus Performance communication network fault.
Open, short to ground, short to voltage, short between bus lines, excessive resistance, connector/wiring fault, module power/ground fault, terminating resistor fault, or network module fault.
Bus naming, topology, connector terminals, wake-up strategy, and expected voltages/resistance are vehicle-specific and must be confirmed in the manufacturer service manual.
check_order: Save all DTCs from every reachable module, freeze-frame data, network topology information, module power/ground status, and communication status before disconnecting components.

Confirm vehicle-specific bus name, network topology, gateway/module locations, connector terminals, terminating resistor locations, voltage/resistance values, and sleep/wake conditions in the manufacturer service manual.
Inspect battery voltage, module powers/grounds, communication wiring, connectors, splices, water intrusion, aftermarket equipment, and bus resistance/voltage under the specified state.
Isolate wiring, connector, gateway, module, termination, and aftermarket-equipment causes using the manufacturer network diagnostic procedure.
common_mistakes: Assuming bus letters, pins, topology, or normal voltage/resistance values are vehicle-universal.
Disconnecting modules randomly without following the manufacturer network isolation order.
Ignoring low battery voltage, water intrusion, aftermarket devices, or module power/ground faults.
required_tools: Compatible scan tool with all-module scan
safety_notes: Use strong caution around SRS, brake, steering, high-voltage, and security/networked modules; disconnecting modules or probing the wrong circuit can disable safety systems or set additional faults, so follow the manufacturer procedure.
source: SAE J2012 / SAE J2012DA generic DTC definitions and Autel official DTC table based diagnostic guidance

Open, short to ground, short to voltage, short between bus lines, excessive resistance, connector/wiring fault, module power/ground fault, terminating resistor fault, or network module fault.
Bus naming, topology, connector terminals, wake-up strategy, and expected voltages/resistance are vehicle-specific and must be confirmed in the manufacturer service manual.
check_order: Save all DTCs from every reachable module, freeze-frame data, network topology information, module power/ground status, and communication status before disconnecting components.

Confirm vehicle-specific bus name, network topology, gateway/module locations, connector terminals, terminating resistor locations, voltage/resistance values, and sleep/wake conditions in the manufacturer service manual.
Inspect battery voltage, module powers/grounds, communication wiring, connectors, splices, water intrusion, aftermarket equipment, and bus resistance/voltage under the specified state.
Isolate wiring, connector, gateway, module, termination, and aftermarket-equipment causes using the manufacturer network diagnostic procedure.
common_mistakes: Assuming bus letters, pins, topology, or normal voltage/resistance values are vehicle-universal.
Disconnecting modules randomly without following the manufacturer network isolation order.
Ignoring low battery voltage, water intrusion, aftermarket devices, or module power/ground faults.
required_tools: Compatible scan tool with all-module scan
safety_notes: Use strong caution around SRS, brake, steering, high-voltage, and security/networked modules; disconnecting modules or probing the wrong circuit can disable safety systems or set additional faults, so follow the manufacturer procedure.
source: SAE J2012 / SAE J2012DA generic DTC definitions and Autel official DTC table based diagnostic guidance
